title: PerceptualComparer::new silently constructs with mismatched dimensions if ref_rgba length does not match width*height*4
description: The constructor accepts arbitrary `ref_rgba`, `width`, and `height` without validating that `ref_rgba.len() == width * height * 4`. If the caller passes a shorter or longer buffer the inner `PerceptualCore::new` receives garbage dimensions, potentially causing out-of-bounds reads inside the kernel. Every other public WASM entry (`downscale_rgb`, `downscale_rgba`, `LookRenderer::new`, `apply_look`, `rotate_rgb8`) performs this check and returns `Err(JsError)`. The constructor cannot return `Result` as-is, so either it should be changed to return `Result<PerceptualComparer, JsError>` or it must panic with a clear message.

title: PerceptualComparer::all_at does not validate that len <= scratch.len(), allowing out-of-bounds slice
description: If `all_at(len)` is called with `len` larger than the staging buffer (e.g. after `input_ptr` was never called, or called with a smaller value), the slice `&buf[..len]` will panic with an index-out-of-bounds. The caller controls `len` from JS. `input_ptr` grows the buffer to at least `len`, but the two calls are not atomically linked — a caller can call `input_ptr(N)`, then call `all_at(N+1)`. A simple `let len = len.min(self.scratch.len())` guard or an explicit error return is needed.

title: frame_stats uses saturating_mul for px then multiplies by 4 without overflow guard, producing a wrong limit on huge inputs
description: `px = width.saturating_mul(height)` correctly saturates to `usize::MAX` on overflow, but `expected = px * 4` immediately afterwards can overflow on 32-bit WASM targets where `usize` is 32 bits and a large `px` multiplied by 4 wraps. The subsequent `limit = pixels.len().min(expected)` would then compute a truncated (wrong) limit, causing `fs_core_trunc_word` to be called with an incorrect `limit` value and potentially returning corrupt stats. The fix is `let expected = px.saturating_mul(4)`.

title: metrics_to_js discards all Reflect::set errors with let _ =, silently producing an empty JS object on failure
description: All three `js_sys::Reflect::set` calls in `metrics_to_js` discard the `Result` with `let _ =`. If `Reflect::set` throws (e.g. the object is non-extensible, or a proxy rejects the set), the returned JsValue will be an object missing some or all fields. The caller (`PerceptualComparer::all`, `all_at`) forwards this silently to JS. A Reflect::set failure is unlikely in normal usage, but callers that depend on the fields being present will silently receive zero/undefined. For a `metrics_to_js` helper this is low risk in practice, but it is still a dropped error.

title: OUT_FULL_16 and OUT_NO_ORIENT share the same bitmask value 8, silently activating both behaviours when either flag is intended
description: Constants `OUT_FULL_16` and `OUT_NO_ORIENT` are both defined as `8` (the same bit). Any caller passing flag `8` intending to request full-resolution 16-bit output will also silently skip orientation, and vice versa. The DNG path explicitly checks `output_flags & OUT_NO_ORIENT` and `output_flags & OUT_FULL_16` separately, so in practice flag 8 activates both branches simultaneously. No runtime error is produced; callers who expect only one behaviour will get the other silently. The fix is to assign distinct bits (e.g. `OUT_FULL_16 = 8`, `OUT_NO_ORIENT = 16`).

title: decode_dng_raw pixel-count overflow check uses checked_mul but then computes w*h unchecked in the error message
description: The guard at line 1741 uses `w.checked_mul(h).unwrap_or(MAX_PIXELS + 1) > MAX_PIXELS` to detect overflow, correctly returning an error on overflow. However, the error message inside the Err branch at line 1744 computes `w * h` without a checked multiply. On 32-bit WASM targets where `usize` is 32 bits, `w * h` can itself overflow and wrap to a small number, producing a misleading error message such as "DNG: 196608 pixels exceeds 50 MP limit" when the actual product overflowed. A parallel bug exists in `decode_cr2_raw` at line 2086-2090.

title: bench_dng uses Path::file_name().unwrap() which panics if path ends in ".."
description: At line 264, `Path::new(path).file_name().unwrap()` panics if `path` ends in `..` or is the root `/`. The same pattern appears in `bench_cr2` (line 335) and `bench_orf` (line 411). While paths passed to these functions are constructed programmatically, any directory path fed via the env vars (GOB_ROOT, P2200_ROOT) ending in a separator or `..` would cause a panic instead of a graceful skip. Using `.unwrap_or_default()` or a fallback like `path` itself is a simple improvement.

title: SMALL_CROP_TIMES Mutex lock failure is silently ignored, dropping crop timing data
description: The lock acquisition `SMALL_CROP_TIMES.lock()` is matched with `if let Ok(mut v)` — a poisoned Mutex (which can happen if a thread panics while holding it) silently discards all crop timing data for that file. The same pattern appears for `LOWLEVEL_PROG_FIRSTS` at line 912. For a benchmark tool this results in missing rows in the parity summary without any diagnostic. A `.expect()` or explicit `eprintln` on the `Err` arm would surface the problem.

title: PerceptualComparer::input_ptr accepts an unbounded len causing unbounded heap growth
description: `input_ptr` grows the scratch buffer to `len` bytes with no upper bound. A JS caller can pass `len = usize::MAX` (or any very large value) causing an OOM in the WASM linear memory. Other WASM entry points that accept buffer sizes validate them against `width*height`. This function has no such guard. Adding a check that `len <= width * height * 4` (stored at construction time) or a reasonable cap (e.g. 256 MB) would prevent a JS caller from crashing the tab.

title: PerceptualComparer::new computes width*height without overflow check before allocating scratch
description: `let n = width * height` at line 2265 can overflow on 32-bit WASM if width and height are both large (e.g. both near 65535). The resulting `n` wraps and `scratch` is allocated to a much smaller (or zero) size than intended. Subsequent `input_ptr(n*4)` or `all_at(n*4)` calls will then panic or silently pass a mismatched length. Using `width.saturating_mul(height)` or a checked multiply with a cap (matching the `validate_orf_structure` limit of 50 MP) would prevent this.

title: OUT_NO_ORIENT flag is not documented in any public API comment, making flag collisions with OUT_FULL_16 invisible to callers
description: The `OUT_NO_ORIENT` constant (value 8, same as `OUT_FULL_16`) is defined with a comment but never mentioned in the public doc-comments for `process_orf_with_flags`, `process_dng_with_flags`, or `process_cr2_with_flags`. Callers who read the public API docs only see flags 1/2/4/8 described for the `_with_flags` variants, and passing 8 intending to get full-res 16-bit also silently skips orientation. This is a documentation gap that increases the likelihood of the dual-meaning constant bug (errors-5) being introduced by future contributors.

title: All WASM error paths produce opaque JsError strings with no structured error codes, making programmatic error handling in JS fragile
description: Every `return Err(JsError::new(&format!(...)))` produces a free-form string. JS callers that want to distinguish decode failures (bad file) from validation failures (too large) from I/O failures must substring-match error messages, which breaks if the message text changes. A structured error type (e.g. an enum serialised to a `{code, message}` JS object, or at minimum a numeric error code prefix in the message string) would let JS callers handle categories robustly without fragile text matching. This is consistent with how libjxl's own error API works.

title: decode_dng_raw dimension check casts usize img.width/height to u32 for MAX_DIM comparison, silently truncating on platforms where usize > u32
description: At lines 1735-1739, `(w as u32) > MAX_DIM` casts `w` (type `usize`, returned from `dng::decode_bytes`) to `u32`. On a 64-bit platform, if `w` is, say, `0x1_0000_0001`, the cast truncates to `1`, passing the check silently. The `validate_orf_structure` function avoids this by comparing `info.width` (which is `u32`) directly against `MAX_DIM: u32`. For DNG, `img.width` and `img.height` are `usize`; the comparison should use `w > MAX_DIM as usize` to avoid silent truncation. The same issue exists in `decode_cr2_raw` (lines 2080-2084).
